coredata.dc:description: Synthetic cathinones, a category of new psychoactive substance (NPS) with diverse structures and appearances in forms such as powder, tablets, and pills, have caused severe social problems. To make them attractive to teenagers, synthetic cathinones may be mixed with instant coffee and fruit juice powders. Difficulties in detecting these drugs in complex matrices complicate seizure of synthetic cathinones. Herein, a novel probe kit comprising hydrophobic tryptophan-carbon dots (htC-dots) with a liquid–liquid extraction procedure was used to detect 4-chloromethcathinone (4-CMC) and other synthetic cathinones mixed into instant coffee and fruit juice powder. Additionally, a self-made fluorescence detector controlled with an Arduino system was developed for the htC-dot probe kits. For 4-CMC, the dynamic ranges and detection limits were assessed as 0.125–0.75 mM and 0.08 mM, respectively, for the microplate reader and as 0.625–5 mM and 0.42 mM, respectively for the self-made fluorescence detector. The sensory mechanism of the probe kit involved photoluminescence (PL) quenching of htC-dots through electron and hot electron transfer under an excitation wavelength of 302 nm. The matrix effects of five brands of instant coffee and fruit juice powder were evaluated, and a cut-off htC-dot solution was prepared to assess htC-dot probe kit accuracy. Finally, 24 real-world samples were tested using the htC-dot probe kits, and the results were confirmed using gas chromatography–mass spectrometer (GC–MS). The results suggested that the htC-dot probe kits have considerable potential for on-site screening of synthetic cathinones mixed into instant coffee and fruit juice powder.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HRTEM images and size distribution and (B) XPS spectrum of htC-dots. (C) Absorption spectra of htC-dots in toluene (0.2 mg mL−1, blue line) and tryptophan (0.1 mM, purple-blue line) in ultrapure water. Inset presents emission spectra of htC-dots (0.05 mg mL−1) in toluene when excited at various wavelengths. (D) Tauc plot of htC-dots in toluene (0.2 mg mL−1). Inset presents Urbach energy plot of htC-dots.

(A) HtC-dot PL quenching efficiencies induced by 4-CMC at excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 500 nm. (B) Sensitivity to 4-CMC and (C) selectivity of htC-dot probe kits. All PL measurements were conducted using a microplate reader. Line colors in (B) from blue to pale blue indicate PL intensities as 4-CMC concentrations increased. The error bars represent standard deviations for three repeated experiments.

(A) Stern–Volmer plots of htC-dots in solutions containing 4-CMC at 0 °C, 25 °C, and 45 °C, respectively. (B) Absorption spectrum of 4-CMC (left) and PL spectrum of htC-dots (right) when excited at 302 nm. Blue arrow indicates spectral signals predicted by prolonging the original spectrum. (C) Cyclic voltammograms of htC-dots (10 mg mL−1) and 4-CMC (10 mM, inset) in DMSO solution at a sweep rate of 0.1 V s−1 under ambient conditions. (D) Lifetime decay curves of htC-dot solutions with and without 4-CMC (1 mM) at emission and excitation wavelengths of 395 and 313 nm, respectively. (E) HtC-dot PL quenching efficiencies induced by 4-CMC (1 mM) plotted as a function of photo energy (eV). (F) Illustration of electron and hot electron transfer between htC-dots and 4-CMC when excited at a 302-nm wavelength. The arrows in (C) indicate the points of the CV signals were used to estimate the oxidation potentials. The error bars represent standard deviations for three repeated experiments.

(A) Key components of the self-made fluorescence detector. (B) Stability tests of htC-dot samples on test and control holders. (C) Relationships between blue fluorescence (FB) values and htC-dots at concentrations of 0.02–0.1 mM on test and control holders. (D) Sensitivity of htC-dots to 4-CMC at concentrations ranging from 0.625 to 5 mM. (E) Selectivity tests for 4-CMC, 4-CEC, eutylone, butylone, ethylone, mephedrone, heroin, ketamine, MA, glucose, sucrose, and fructose. The error bars represent standard deviations for three repeated experiments.

(A) Matrix effect per 200 mg of five instant coffee and fruit juice powder samples detected by a PL spectrometer (blue) and self-made fluorescence detector (pink). (B) PL images of htC-dot probe kits irradiated with a portable UV lamp at an excitation wavelength of 302 nm. (C) PL images of each htC-dot probe kit after screening for 4-CMC-doping in coffee and fruit juice powder (w/w: 1 %) and corresponding cut-off htC-dot solutions excited using a portable 302-nm UV lamp. Numbers displayed on the self-made fluorescence detector represent blue fluorescence (FB) values for each htC-dot probe (test) after screening and the corresponding cut-off htC-dot solution (control). The error bars represent standard deviations for three repeated experiments.

Practicality tests of four representative samples and detection results. Images for samples 1–4 include (a) sample photograph, (b) Raman detection results, and (c and d) screening results of htC-dot probe (test) and corresponding cut-off htC-dot solution (control) using a portable UV lamp at an excitement wavelength of 302 and the self-made fluorescence detector, respectively.
Process for screening real-world samples using htC-dot probe kits.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthetic cathinones, a category of new psychoactive substance (NPS) with diverse structures and appearances in forms such as powder, tablets, and pills, have caused severe social problems. To make them attractive to teenagers, synthetic cathinones may be mixed with instant coffee and fruit juice powders. Difficulties in detecting these drugs in complex matrices complicate seizure of synthetic cathinones. Herein, a novel probe kit comprising hydrophobic tryptophan-carbon dots (htC-dots) with a liquid–liquid extraction procedure was used to detect 4-chloromethcathinone (4-CMC) and other synthetic cathinones mixed into instant coffee and fruit juice powder. Additionally, a self-made fluorescence detector controlled with an Arduino system was developed for the htC-dot probe kits. For 4-CMC, the dynamic ranges and detection limits were assessed as 0.125–0.75 mM and 0.08 mM, respectively, for the microplate reader and as 0.625–5 mM and 0.42 mM, respectively for the self-made fluorescence detector. The sensory mechanism of the probe kit involved photoluminescence (PL) quenching of htC-dots through electron and hot electron transfer under an excitation wavelength of 302 nm. The matrix effects of five brands of instant coffee and fruit juice powder were evaluated, and a cut-off htC-dot solution was prepared to assess htC-dot probe kit accuracy. Finally, 24 real-world samples were tested using the htC-dot probe kits, and the results were confirmed using gas chromatography–mass spectrometer (GC–MS). The results suggested that the htC-dot probe kits have considerable potential for on-site screening of synthetic cathinones mixed into instant coffee and fruit juice powder.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Instant coffee and fruit juice powders

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: New psychoactive substances (NPSs) are psychotropic drugs that are not controlled by the United Nations 1961 Single Convention on Narcotic Drugs and 1971 Convention on Psychotropic Substances [1,2]. Stimulant NPSs include synthetic cathinones, oxazolines, phenethylamines, phenidates, phenylmorpholines, and piperazines, with approximately 400 individual compounds have been reported by United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC) [2]. Additionally, a report from the European Union Drugs Agency (EUDA) revealed that the institution was monitoring 167 varieties of synthetic cathinones, substances often illegally abused for recreational purposes [3,4].

Cathinone and its derivatives have β-ketone phenethylamine structures with similar neurophysiological functions to those of amphetamine, interacting with catecholamine neurotransmitters, particularly dopaminergic (DA) and norepinephrinergic systems [4–6]. Synthetic cathinones reduce dopamine receptor antagonist and DA release inhibitor activity [7,8]. The overdose effects of these cathinones include tachycardia, hyperthermia, insomnia, agitation, hallucinations, delusions, and confusion, which can lead to aggressive behavior or even fatality [9–11].

Synthetic cathinones, such as 4-chloromethcathinone (4-CMC), 4-chloroethcathinone (4-CEC), eutylone, butylone, ethylone, and mephedrone, commonly appear as white or yellow powder or crystals in HCl form and are administered through smoking, mucosal delivery, or intramuscular injection, although they may also be insufflated or swallowed in tablets [5,12,13]. Synthetic cathinones mixed with instant coffee powder, general called as “toxic coffee packets,” have been popular in Taiwan since 2017 [13–16]. Mixtures of these drugs with instant fruit juice powder have also emerged [17]. On-site screening of synthetic cathinones at crime scenes is a critical procedure for law enforcement officers before they seize suspicious powders [18,19]. If screening results are positive, the powders are subsequently sent to laboratories for confirmation. Although gas chromatography–mass spectrometer (GC–MS) and liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometer (LC–MS/MS) are generally used to analyze illicit drugs, these instructments are not suitable for preliminary testing because of their low cost-efficiency and a lack of expert operators [20–22].

Carbon dots (C-dots) have attracted widespread attention because they are easy to produce and have a low cost, unique photophysical properties, and high biocompatibility [23–25]. They have been applied for sensors, electronic devices, adsorbents, energy storage, catalysis, cell imaging, and cancer therapy [26–28]. C-dot-functionalized papers (CDFPs) for synthetic cathinone sensors have been developed with arginine used as a precursor to form C-dots, which are then combined with wax-printed papers. CDFPs have been applied to detect synthetic cathinones with high purity or mixed with common additives, such as glucose [29]. However, research on screening synthetic cathinones in complex matrices, such as coffee and fruit powder, remains limited.

In the present study, C-dot probe kits were developed for detection of synthetic cathinones mixed with instant coffee or fruit powder. In addition, a self-made fluorescence detector was validated and then employed with the C-dot probe kits for automated identification. The results indicated that C-dot probe kits with a portable ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) lamp or the self-made fluorescence detector had 100 % accuracy in screening 24 real-world seized samples, suggesting these kits have high potential to combat drug-related crime.

Ferrocene, fructose, glucose, sucrose, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), tryptophan, tetrabutyl ammonium hexafluorophosphate, and 2-aminopyridine were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Milwaukee, WI, USA). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and toluene were purchased from J.T. Baker (Phillipsburg, NJ, USA). Ultrapure water (18.2 mΩ·cm) supplied by an ultrapure water system (Direct-Q3, Merck Millipore, Billerica, MA, USA) was utilized to prepare all aqueous solutions. Commonly abused drugs, including heroin, ketamine, and methamphetamine (MA), and synthetic cathinones, including 4-chloromethcathinone (4-CMC), 4-chloroethcathinone (4-CEC), eutylone, butylone, ethylone, and mephedrone, were provided in their highly pure hydrochloride salt forms by the Investigation Bureau, Ministry of Justice, Taiwan. Various brands and varieties of instant coffee and fruit juice powder, namely Mr. Brown coffee (Mandheling blend, Mr. Brown Coffee Company, Taipei City, Taiwan), Nescafe coffee (Gold blend, Nestlé, Vevey, Switzerland), Maxwell House coffee (Supreme, Maxwell House, Walla Walla, WA, USA), and American Sweet Orange and Sweet Mango NutriSari fruit juice powder (PT. Nutrifood Indonesia, Bogor City, Indonesia), were purchased from a local supermarket.

Hydrophobic tryptophan-carbon dots (htC-dots) were synthesized through a solvothermal route, in alignment with previous studies [30–32]. To prepare the htC-dots, 0.2 g of tryptophan were added to a Teﬂon-lined stainless-steel autoclave containing 20 mL of toluene and heated in a furnace at 240 °C for 14 h. After cooling to room temperature (25 °C), the resulting brown-yellow solution was filtered through a 0.22-μm polytetrafluoroethylene membrane to remove large particles. The obtained solution was then purified using a 2 N NaOH aqueous solution to eliminate any unreacted reactant and water-soluble byproducts from reaction with the base until no fluorescence was detected in the extractant with a portable UV lamp (302-nm emission wavelength, UVLM-26, Analytik Jena, Germany) [33]. The purified solution was dried and redissolved in 20 mL of toluene to prepare a purified htC-dot solution, which was stored at 4 °C in a dark room prior to use. After the solution (1 mL) had evaporated, the residual product was weighed, and the concentration of htC-dots in the solution was determined to be 8.0 mg mL−1.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of the htC-dots deposited on 400-mesh carbon-coated Cu grids were captured using a transmission electron microscopy instrument (JEM-2100F, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) operated at 200 kV. Surface elements and bonding were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with a device purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (ESCALAB Xi+, Waltham, MA, USA). The absorption spectra of the htC-dots in toluene (0.2 mg mL−1), tryptophan in ultrapure water (0.1 mM), and 4-CMC in methanol (0.1 mM) were recorded using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Evolution 220) purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, MA, USA). A microplate reader from Molecular Devices (SpectraMax i3x, San Jose, CA, USA) was used to measure the PL spectra of the htC-dots in toluene at different excitation wavelengths. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the htC-dots and tryptophan were recorded using a spectrometer from Thermo Scientific (Nicolet iS5, Waltham, MA, USA). The fluorescence quantum yields (Φf) of the htC-dots in toluene at excitation wavelengths of 302 nm were calculated using 2-aminopyridine dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a standard (Φf = 0.60). The absorbance values of the htC-dot solution and 2-aminopyridine in cuvettes were kept under 0.1 at their excitation wavelength to minimize the re-absorption effect.

To determine the energy gap (Eg) of the htC-dots, the Eg value was estimated using Tauc’s law, as presented in Eq. (1) [34–36]:
(1) (αhν)2 = k (hν–Eg)

where α denotes the absorption coeﬃcient, hν denotes the energy of the incident light, and k is the constant. Extrapolation of the straight line on its Tauc plot produced the estimated Eg value. Next, the Urbach energy (Eu) was used to investigate htC-dot disorders or defects by applying Eq. (2) [34,37,38]:
(2) α(hν)= α0 exp(hν/Eu)

where α denotes the absorption coeﬃcient, α0 is the constant, and hν denotes the energy of the incident light. The Eu values were estimated using the reciprocal of slopes in the lower-energy region based on their ln α(hν) vs. hν plots.

An aliquot (250 μL) of the stock htC-dot solution was diluted in 19.75 mL of toluene to prepare an htC-dot probe solution. Subsequently, 2 mL of alkaline aqueous solution (2 N NaOH) and 2 mL of the htC-dot probe solution were added to a glass tube (9 mL) to form a two-layer solution for the htC-dot probe kit (4 mL). To determine the optimal excitation wavelength, htC-dot PL quenching efficiencies induced by 4-CMC (5 mM) against various excitation wavelengths, ranging from 300 to 500 nm, were measured. Briefly, aliquots (100 μL) of prepared samples with and without 4-CMC (205 mM) in ultrapure water were mixed with the htC-dot probe kits. After being shaken by hand for 30 s and left to settle for 3 min, the upper toluene sensing layers (250 μL) of the htC-dot probe kits were transferred to a 96-well SpectraPlate before PL measurement using a microplate reader. The PL spectra of the htC-dots in both solutions were recorded when excited at wavelengths between 300 and 500 nm. Consequently, an excitation wavelength of 302 nm was selected for further analysis.

To evaluate the sensitivity of htC-dots to 4-CMC at a 302-nm excitation wavelength, aliquots (100 μL) of prepared samples containing various concentrations (0–205 mM) of 4-CMC in ultrapure water were separately mixed with htC-dot probe kits. Preprocessing was completed following the same procedures used for excitation wavelength assessment, but PL spectra were only recorded at a wavelength of 302 nm. PL quenching efficiencies at an emission wavelength of 395 nm were plotted against the analyte with final concentrations of 0.125–5 mM to evaluate assay sensitivity. Selectivity tests were performed following similar procedures to those in the 4-CMC solution analysis but with prepared samples (205 mM) containing commonly abused drugs, namely heroin, ketamine, MA, 4-CMC, 4-CEC, eutylone, butylone, ethylone, and mephedrone, and common additives, namely glucose, fructose, and sucrose. All analytes with a final concentration of 5 mM were used for assessment, and their chemical structures are provided in Fig. S1. All experiments were performed under ambient conditions and were repeated for triplicates.

To study the quenching mechanism between the htC-dots and 4-CMC, PL quenching of the htC-dots induced by 4-CMC was conducted on samples at 0, 25, and 45 °C. The Stern–Volmer equation was used to assess PL quenching behavior, as presented in Eq. (3) [39–42]:
(3)
F

1

K
sv
[ Q ]
where F0 and F denote the PL intensities of the htC-dots in the absence and presence of the quencher, respectively. [Q] denotes the concentration of quencher (4-CMC), and KSV denotes the corresponding Stern–Volmer constant. In addition, a three-electrode configuration consisting of a glassy carbon working electrode, a KCl-saturated Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and a platinum wire counter electrode was used for collecting cyclic voltammetry measurements. Cyclic voltammograms of htC-dots (10 mg mL−1), 4-CMC (10 mM), and ferrocene (1 mM) in DMSO solution containing tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (0.1 M) were recorded at a sweep rate of 0.1 V s−1. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) levels of htC-dots and 4-CMC were estimated using Eqs. (4) and (5) [29,32,42]:
(4) EHOMO = − (4.8 + EOX − EOX,Fc/Fc +)
(5) ELUMO = EHOMO + E0–0onset /Eg

where EOX denotes the oxidation potential of the tested chemicals, which was determined using a CHI 611E electrochemical workstation purchased from CH instruments (Austin, TX, USA). EOX,Fc/Fc + denotes the oxidation potential of ferrocene/ferrocene+; E0–0onset denotes the 0–0 energy, defined as the lowest energy transition estimated from the longest absorption wavelength (λonset) of 4-CMC; and Eg denotes the energy bandgap of the htC-dots, estimated using the Tauc plot [34–36]. The E0–0onset value of 4-CMC (5
×
10−5 M in methanol) was measured on the basis of absorption spectra. The Eg value of the htC-dots (0.2 mg mL−1 in toluene) was also recorded and then transformed into a Tauc plot to determine Eg. We note that turning points of the CV signals, rather than onset points, were used to estimate the oxidation potentials, in order to minimize the ambiguity arising from the exponential behavior of partial currents, as described in the Butler–Volmer formalism. A steady-state PL spectrometer equipped with a 313-nm picosecond pulsed LED (FS5, Edinburgh Instruments, Kirkton Campus, UK) at an emission wavelength of 395 nm was used to measure the lifetime of the htC-dot probe solutions with and without 4-CMC (1 mM).

A self-made fluorescence detector was designed using SolidWorks software and created with a 3D printer through fused deposition modeling. The key components of the detector included an LCD Monitor (5 V HD44780U 1602 LCD, Hitachi, Japan), color sensors (TCS34725, Taiwan Intelligent Sensor Technology, Tainan, Taiwan), a UV pass filter (10356, AliExpress, China), and a 302-nm UV lamp (6 W, UVP 34004401, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, USA), that were all purchased from local parts stores. Operation was controlled using an Arduino board (Uno R3, Arduino, Italy) [43–46]. Toluene and htC-dot solutions with a concentration of 0.1 mg mL−1 were used to calibrate the self-made fluorescence detector on their blue fluorescence (FB) values to 0 and 100, respectively. To test the stability of the fluorescence detector, eight batches of htC-dot solution (250 μL, 0.1 mg mL−1) and separate htC-dot solutions of varying concentrations (0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, and 0.1 mg mL−1) in vials were measured using the detector. Additionally, the sensitivity and selectivity of the htC-dots were also measured using the fluorescence detector and compared with the results obtained through PL measurement using a microplate reader. All experiments were performed under ambient conditions and were repeated for triplicates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Instant coffee and fruit juice powder matrix interference
ce:section.ce:para: Five brands of instant coffee and fruit juice powder purchased from a supermarket in Taiwan—Mr. Brown coffee (Mandheling blend), Nescafe coffee (gold blend), Maxwell House coffee (Supreme, Maxwell House), and American Sweet Orange and Sweet Mango NutriSari fruit juice powder—were used to study matrix interference. A real-world sample (200 mg) of each coffee and fruit juice powder was added to the htC-dot probe kits. After being shaken for 30 s and left to settle for 3 min, the upper sensing layer (250 μL) of each in htC-dot probe kit was transferred to a 96-well plate and PL spectra at an excitation wavelength of 302 nm were recorded (n = 3). To diminish the matrix effect and increase accuracy, the htC-dot probe solution was diluted in toluene to obtain a cut-off solution (0.08 mg mL−1). Aliquots (250 μL) of this solution were transferred into vials and used as cut-off controls.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of 4-CMC doped in instant coffee and fruit juice powder
ce:section.ce:para: Real-world samples of 4-CMC-doped coffee and fruit juice powder (w/w: 1 % and 3 %) were prepared by mixing 4-CMC with powders from the five aforementioned brands. These five samples (w/w: 1 %) were tested using the htC-dot probe kits and cut-off htC-dot vials to assess practicality by following a similar procedure to that used in the instant coffee and fruit juice powder matrix interference assessment. Approximately 200 mg of each 4-CMC-doped coffee and fruit juice powder (w/w: 1 %) was added to a htC-dot probe kit before being shaken for 30 s and left to settle for 3 min. Subsequently, 250 μL of the probe kit sensing layer was transferred to a 1-mL glass vial (C4008–50, Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) and then irradiated and analyzed alongside a cut-off htC-dot vial by using a portable UV lamp and self-made fluorescence detector measurement, respectively. In addition, 200 mg of each doped coffee and fruit juice powder (w/w: 3 %) was screened for 4-CMC by using a handheld Raman analyzer (ResQ FLX, Rigaku Corporation, Tokyo, Japan) for comparison with the htC-dot probe kit results. The Raman spectral range was 200–2500 cm−1 with a full-width-at-half-maximum resolution of 8 cm−1.

Twenty-four real-world samples seized by law enforcement officers were tested using the htC-dot probe kits and cut-off htC-dot vials to evaluate the practicality and accuracy of the kits. The assessment followed the same procedure as that used for the 4-CMC-doped instant coffee and fruit juice powders. A handheld Raman device was also used to analyze the samples for comparison, and final confirmation of the results was achieved by analyzing the probe kit sensing layers with a gas chromatography–mass spectrometer (Agilent 8890/5977B MSD/GC, GC–MS).

The HRTEM images displayed in Fig. 1(A) indicate that the htC-dots had spherical structures, with average diameters of 5.9 ± 1.7 nm (from 38 counts) [47,48]. The inset of Fig. 1(A) displays the crystallized carbogenic core of the htC-dot, with a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm that corresponds to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon, which aligns with the findings of prior studies [49,50]. The htC-dots were well-dispersed and stable in toluene solution but non-dispersed in water, confirming their hydrophobicity. Fig. 1(B) displays three major peaks obtained at 284, 400, and 532 eV in the XPS spectra of the htC-dots, with these peaks corresponding to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, with atomic percentages of 71.9 %, 7.6 %, and 14.0 %, respectively [51–53]. Fig. S2(A) depicts the deconvoluted C 1 s peaks obtained at 284.7, 285.9, and 288.5 eV, with these peaks corresponding to C–C/C–H, C–O/C–N, and CO bindings [52,53]. The deconvoluted XPS spectra of O 1 s, displayed in Fig. S2(B), reveal two peaks at 531.6 and 532.1 eV that were attributed to CO and C–O bindings, respectively [52,53]. Fig. S2(C) presents the deconvoluted XPS spectra of N 1 s, revealing one peak at 399.8 eV that was attributed to C–N bonding [52,54]. The FTIR spectra of the htC-dots and their precursor tryptophan displayed in Fig. S3 indicate slight differences between signals. Tryptophan exhibited a sharp O–H/N–H vibrational stretching band at 3400 cm−1, a moderate CO stretching band at 1665 cm−1, a sharp CC stretching band in the aromatic ring at 1600 cm−1, and a sharp CC stretching band in the indole aromatic ring at 1354 cm−1. After the htC-dots formed, they exhibited a weak O–H vibrational stretching band at 3420 cm−1 and a broad CC/CO stretching band in the aromatic ring at 1624 cm−1 [32].

Fig. 1(C) displays the absorption spectra of the htC-dots in toluene solution (0.2 mg mL−1), which exhibited a shape absorption signal at 290 nm, broad bands between 300 and 400 nm, and a tail between 400–450 nm, corresponding to π−π* and n − π* transitions, respectively, in contrast to the spectrum of tryptophan in methanol (10−4 M) [29,32,55]. The inset in Fig. 1(C) displays the PL spectra of the htC-dots, which exhibited non-excitation-wavelength-dependent emission at approximately 395 nm when excited at wavelengths of 300–340 nm, originating primarily from the htC-dot cores. Contrastly, htC-dots exhibited excitation-wavelength-dependent PL properties when excited by wavelengths ranging from 365–450 nm. This behavior is ascribed to the transitions from π* orbitals back to their original nonbonding orbitals in the htC-dots as well as their varieties of energy gaps coming from different htC-dot sizes [56,57]. A moderate fluorescent quantum yield (17.5 %) was obtained when the htC-dots were excited at 302 nm. The Eg of the htC-dots was estimated as 3.30 eV on the basis of the Tauc plot depicted in Fig. 1(D) [58,59]. The inset in Fig. 1(D) presents the Eu value of the htC-dots, which was estimated to be 277 meV. This Eu value is lower than those of other C-dots reported in the literature, indicating that the htC-dots in the present study exhibited more nanostructured features with fewer defects [34,37,38].

Fig. 2(A) presents PL quenching efficiencies at htC-dot maximum emission peaks induced by 4-CMC at excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 500 nm. The PL quenching efficiencies increased from 0 % to 77.5 % as the excitation wavelength decreased, indicating that higher excitation energy led to increased PL quenching efficiency. Thus, a portable UV lamp capable of emitting 302-nm wavelength was integrated into our sensing system, and this excitation wavelength was selected for further study. Fig. 2(B) depicts the PL intensities of the sensing layers in htC-dot probe kits with and without 4-CMC at various concentrations (0.125–5 mM). When excited at 302 nm, 4-CMC induced PL quenching of the htC-dots at an emission wavelength of approximately 395 nm, depending on the concentration. As displayed in the Fig. 2(B) inset, a strong linear relationship (R2 = 0.996) of the (F0 − F)/F0 of htC-dots against 4-CMC at concentrations ranging from 0.125 to 0.75 mM was observed. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 0.08 mM, as calculated using the formula 3δ/m, in which δ represents the deviation of (F0 − F)/F0 for the blank test and m represents the slope of the calibration plot. Fig. 2(C) depicts the results of selectivity tests for commonly abused drugs, including heroin, ketamine, and MA, and common additives, including glucose, sucrose, and fructose. Whereas these drugs and additives did not induce PL quenching of the htC-dots, synthetic cathinones, including 4-CMC, 4-CEC, eutylone, butylone, ethylone, and mephedrone, induced over 60 % PL quenching, indicating that the htC-dot probe kits were highly selective to the main chemical structure of synthetic cathinones.

Coffee and fruit juice powders were screened for synthetic cathinones by using htC-dot probe kits consisting of an htC-dot probe solution (upper layer) and alkaline aqueous solution (lower layer). After screening, major coffee and fruit juice powder matrices were left in the alkaline aqueous solution, and synthetic cathinones extracted using the toluene were reacted with the htC-dots in the probe solution. To assess the quenching mechanism, 4-CMC was selected among the synthetic cathinones for further testing. Fig. 3(A) displays the Stern–Volmer plots (R2 = 0.99) of the htC-dots against 4-CMC at 0 °C, 25 °C, and 45 °C, with Stern–Volmer constants (KSV) of 0.21, 0.23, and 0.35 mM−1, respectively. Higher KSV values were observed as temperature increased, with the results indicating that dynamic quenching occurred between the htC-dots and 4-CMC [39–42]. Fig. 3(B) indicates that the absorption spectrum of the 4-CMC did not overlap with the PL spectrum of the htC-dots, ruling out PL resonance energy transfer as an explanation [29,42]. As presented in Fig. 3(C), the oxidation potentials of the htC-dots and 4-CMC were estimated to be 0.59 and 1.69 eV, respectively [29], and the oxidation potential of ferrocene/ferrocene+ was estimated to be 0.58 eV (Fig. S4) [42]. The HOMO values of the htC-dots and 4-CMC were calculated as −4.81 and −5.91 eV, respectively, by using Eq. (4). The LUMO values of the htC-dots and 4-CMC were thus calculated as −1.51 and −1.78 eV, respectively, by using Eq. (5), with Eg values (3.30 and 4.13 eV, respectively) estimated based on the Tauc plot and absorption spectra presented in Fig. 1(D) and Fig. S5, respectively [29,42]. The higher LUMO level of the htC-dots relative to that of the 4-CMC indicated electron transfer occurrence from the htC-dots to the 4-CMC [29]. Fig. 3(D) displays the lifetime decay curves of htC-dots with and without 4-CMC (1 mM) at emission and excitation wavelengths of 395 and 313 nm, respectively, with a shorter decay curve for the htC-dots containing 4-CMC. The htC-dots without 4-CMC had lifetimes of 1.7 ns (τ1, 84.4 %) and 4.6 ns (τ2, 15.6 %), and the htC-dots containing 4-CMC had lifetimes of 1.6 ns (τ1, 80.0 %) and 3.8 ns (τ2, 20.0 %). The reduced lifetime in the htC-dots with 4-CMC indicated that the LUMO of htC-dots was involved in the electron transfer between the htC-dots and 4-CMC [29]. Fig. 3(E) indicates that the PL quenching efﬁciency increased as a function of excitation energy, suggesting hot energy transfer [60–62]. Thus, electron transfer from htC-dots to 4-CMC could easily be achieved at higher-energy excitations. The PL quenching efficiency of the htC-dots when irradiated with approximately 3.3 eV of photo energy was estimated to be 0.35. The PL quenching efficiency increased to 0.65 as the photo energy increased up to 3.75 eV and then remained stable as the photo energy increased from 3.75 to 4.00 eV. At 4.13 eV (Ex 300 nm), the PL quenching efficiency reached 0.77, indicating that photoelectrons in the htC-dots could be excited to higher LUMO levels (LUMO+N), leading to hot electron transfer from htC-dots to 4-CMC [60,61]. Thus, irradiating the htC-dots and 4-CMC at a 302-nm excitation wavelength likely resulted in both electron and hot electron transfer, as depicted in Fig. 3(F).

To further elaborate on photoinduced electron transfer (PET) in C-dots, it is theoretically feasible that when the LUMO levels of analytes are lower than those of C-dots, electron transfer can be facilitated under appropriate UV irradiation, consistent with findings in previous literature [63–65]. In other words, analytes with higher (more positive) reduction potentials exhibit a greater tendency to accept electrons from LUMO of C-dots. Conversely, selectivity can be achieved when the LUMO levels of C-dots are lower than those of non-target species, as the latter typically possess lower reduction potentials, thereby making electron transfer thermodynamically unfavorable and effectively suppressing the process [29,65]. Additionally, the use of a tunable irradiation wavelength, as demonstrated in Fig. 2(A), serves as a driving force to modulate PET behavior, affecting sensitivity and selectivity. In general, shorter irradiation wavelengths result in higher quenching efficiencies, which may enhance sensitivity but reduce selectivity. It is noteworthy that analytes with similar or higher reduction potentials exhibit comparable electron-accepting abilities, which may limit the selectivity of electron transfer-based sensing. Therefore, as an alternative approach, polarity differences between C-dots and non-targets can be exploited to improve selectivity. In the present work, synthetic cathinones are selectively extracted into the upper toluene sensing layer under alkaline aqueous conditions, while the majority of non-target substances and matrix components from instant coffee and fruit juice powders remain in the aqueous layer, thereby minimizing interference during detection.

The self-made fluorescence detector depicted in Fig. 4(A) included several key components, namely sample holders, a 302-nm UV lamp, a UV pass filter, color sensors, and a display screen. Vials containing the htC-dot probe and cut-off htC-dot solution were inserted into the test and control sample holders, respectively. A 302-nm UV lamp with a UV pass filter was used as the excitation source. After both htC-dot samples were excited, their FB was detected using color sensors, and after signal transformation, FB values were presented on the display screen. The FB values of the self-made fluorescence detector were calibrated to 0 and 100 using toluene and htC-dots (0.1 mg mL⁻¹), respectively, through an Arduino system board.

Fig. 4(B) provides the FB values of the test and control htC-dot samples from eight batches. The FB values for both samples were approximately 100, with error values from −5.5 % to 2.3 % (test sample) and −4.6 % to 1.7 % (control sample), indicating stability. The FB values exhibited strong and similar linear relationships (R2 = 0.996) with the test and control htC-dot samples at concentrations ranging from 0.02–0.1 mM, as displayed in Fig. 4(C). Assessments performed using the self-made fluorescence detector indicated a similar function to that of a PL spectrometer. Fig. 4(D) displays the sensitivity of the htC-dots to 4-CMC at concentrations ranging from 0.625 to 5 mM. The LOD was calculated as 0.42 mM by using the formula 3δ/m, in which δ denotes the deviation of (FB0 − FB)/FB0 for the blank test and m denotes the slope of the calibration plot. The htC-dots exhibited lower sensitivity to the 4-CMC detected by the self-made fluorescence detector compared with that obtained through PL measurement, with this result attributable to the limits of the self-made device built using low-cost electronic components purchased from local parts stores. Selectivity tests performed using the self-made fluorescence detector, the results of which are displayed in Fig. 4(E), indicated that 4-CMC, 4-CEC, eutylone, butylone, ethylone, and mephedrone induced PL quenching over 60 %, whereas heroin, ketamine, MA, glucose, sucrose, and fructose did not induce PL quenching. These results were similar to those obtained through PL measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Instant coffee and fruit juice powder matrix interference
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5(A) displays the matrix interference per 200 mg of five samples of instant coffee and fruit juice powder—orange and mango NutriSari fruit juice powder, Maxwell House instant coffee powder, Mr. Brown instant coffee powder, and Nescafe instant coffee powder—as detected by a PL spectrometer and the self-made fluorescence detector. The powder matrices induced PL at a wavelength of 395 nm and FB quenching, with matrix effects measured as −5.4 % ± 6.4 % and 6.3 % ± 6.8 % (Nutrisari-Orange), −4.0 % ± 8.7 % and 11.5 % ± 5.0 % (Nutrisari-Mango), 10.6 % ± 10.1 % and 14.8 % ± 3.2 % (Maxwell), 28.5 % ± 5.4 % and 19.4 % ± 2.8 % (Mr. Brown), and 19.3 % ± 8.9 % and 17.4 % ± 2.8 % (Nescafe), respectively. After employing a liquid-liquid extraction, the upper toluene sensing layer of the htC-dot probe kits was transferred into vials. Fluorescence images, displayed in Fig. 5(B), revealed no significant differences between the htC-dot vials that are noticeable to the naked eye, primarily because the major coffee and fruit juice powder matrices were left in the alkaline aqueous solution of the htC-dot probe kits, and only partial PL quenching was visible. Vials of cut-off htC-dot (0.08 mg mL−1) solution with an FB value of approximately 80 were used as cut-off controls for assessments performed using the self-made fluorescence detector based on the highest matrix effect (approximately 19.4 %) induced by the instant coffee powder samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of 4-CMC in instant coffee and fruit juice powder
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5(C), (a) to (e), provides PL images of five htC-dot probe vials after the screening of five samples of 4-CMC-doped coffee and fruit juice powder (w/w: 1 %) and corresponding cut-off htC-dot vials irradiated at a wavelength of 302 nm by a portable UV lamp. The screening results from the self-made fluorescence detector were also recorded. The PL intensities of the htC-dot probes were lower than those of the cut-off htC-dot solutions, indicating positive test results for synthetic cathinones. FB values for the five tests and control samples were recorded as 81 and 22 (NutriSari-Orange), 82 and 27 (NutriSari-Mango), 77 and 24 (Maxwell), 80 and 22 (Mr. Brown), 78 and 25 (Nescafe), respectively. These values indicate that the commercial UV lamp and self-made fluorescence detector provided consistent screening results.

To enable comparison, a handheld Raman analyzer was used to detect synthetic cathinones at a 4-CMC concentration of 3 % in coffee and fruit juice powder. As displayed in (A) of Fig. S6 to S10, four tests identified the samples as sugar, with high quality scores of 0.91–0.96, and one test showed 2-bromo-4,5-dimethoxyphenetylamine but with a low quality score of 0.78 (under the stetted value of 0.8), according to the hit quality index and a proprietary Rigaku Wavelet algorithm that evaluates library databases [66,67]. The Raman signals of the five samples containing 3 % 4-CMC were similar to those of their corresponding matrix, as displayed in (B) of Fig. S6 to S10. These results suggest that the complicated matrix in the five instant coffee and fruit juice powder samples interfered with the sample detection conducted using a handheld Raman device, and the htC-dot probe kits had better detection capabilities.

Scheme 1 illustrates the process for screening real-world samples by using htC-dot probe kits. A suspicious coffee packet sample found at crime scenes was weighted and sampled (approximately 200 mg) using a portable mass balance. The mixture was then added to the htC-dot probe kits and shaken gently for 1 min. After solvent separation, the 250-μL upper toluene sensing layer was transferred into an empty vial. Test and cut-off htC-dot vials were then irradiated using a portable UV lamp or the self-made fluorescence detector.

To assess the practicality and accuracy of the detection method, 24 real-world samples were screened using a handheld Raman device and the htC-dot probe kits. Fig. 6 displays images of representative samples (1−4) and their detection results. Use of the handheld Raman device resulted in detection of nothing for sample 1, mephedrone (0.94) for sample 2, hexamethylsilathiane (0.51) for sample 3, and dextrose monohydrate (0.97) for sample 4, which indicates that only sample 2 tested positive for synthetic cathinones. The self-made fluorescence detector revealed FB values of 77 and 8 (sample 1), 79 and 14 (sample 2), 79 and 23 (sample 3), and 82 and 29 (sample 4) for the control and test samples, respectively, indicating all four samples were positive for synthetic cathinones. To confirm these results, after screening, GC–MS analysis was performed on each of the htC-dot probe solutions. Supplementary Material I provides further details on the total ion chromatography (TIC) and corresponding mass spectra of analytes for the four samples, and mass spectrum matching results were obtained using the SWGDRUG Mass Spectral Library (Version 3.13) and Cayman Spectral Library (SEPT. 2024) [68,69]. TIC peaks were observed at 2.74 min for sample 1 (corresponding to mephedrone), 2.74 min for sample 2 (mephedrone), 2.74 min for sample 3 (mephedrone), and 2.88 min for sample 4 (4-CMC). Thus, the screening results for samples 1–4 were consistent between the htC-dot probe kits and GC–MS measurements. By contrast, the handheld Raman device identified only sample 2 as containing mephedrone, likely because of interference from complicated matrices in instant coffee and fruit juice powders. The other 20 real-world samples and their detection results are provided in Fig. S11 to S30 and Supplementary Material I. These samples were all identified as containing synthetic cathinones by using the htC-dot probe kits, but only sample 21 was determined to contain methylone (MDMC) when a handheld Raman analyzer was used. Overall, the htC-dot kits exhibited 100 % (24/24) screening accuracy in 24 real-world samples, which is higher than the accuracy of 8.3 % (2/24) achieved using a handheld Raman analyzer.

The present study demonstrated the application of a novel htC-dot probe kit equipped with a portable UV lamp and self-made fluorescence detector for screening 4-CMC and other synthetic cathinones doped in instant coffee and fruit juice powders. When irradiated with a UV lamp at an excitation wavelength of 302 nm, 4-CMC induced PL quenching of the htC-dots in the probe kits through electron and hot electron transfer. The screening capabilities of the self-made fluorescence detector were also validated. The matrix effects of five brands of instant coffee and fruit juice powder were evaluated. Thus, a cut-off htC-dot solution was prepared to promote the practicality and accuracy of the htC-dot probe kits. A total of 24 real-world samples were tested using the probe kits and verified through GC–MS analyses. The results indicated that the htC-dot probe kits exhibited considerable potential for use in on-site screening of synthetic cathinones mixed into instant coffee and fruit powders.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yao-Te Yen is an investigator in the Department of Forensic Science, Ministry of Justice Investigation Bureau, Taiwan. He has been working for development of analytic methods in illicit drugs for decades. He received his MS degree from the Department of Chemistry at National Tsing Hua University in 2007. He also received his PhD degree at National Taiwan University in 2021 in prof. Huan-Tsung Chang’s group. He is interested in mass spectrometry, isotope ratio mass spectrometry, nanomaterials, and sensors.

Huai-Han Chang obtained his M.S. degree from the Institute of Applied Mechanics, National Taiwan University. His research interests include biochemical sensors, semiconductor manufacturing, and optic signal detection system development.

Yu-Ti Wang is a research assistant in the Department of Forensic Science, Ministry of Justice Investigation Bureau, Taiwan. She obtained her BS degree from Applied Chemistry, National Yang Ming Chiao Tung University.

Wei-Ting Chen is current a Ph.D student of the Prof. Chen’s research group in the Institute of Applied Mechanics, National Taiwan University. He obtained his M.S. degree from the Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry at the National Chung Cheng University. His research interests include surface chemistry, nanomaterial synthesis, and biosensors.

Tzu-Ching Lin is a Research Assistant in the Department of Forensic Science at the Ministry of Justice Investigation Bureau in Taiwan. She has been working on the development of lateral flow immunochromatographic assays for detecting illicit drugs. She obtained her M.S. degree from the Institute of Biopharmaceutical Sciences at the National Yang Ming Chiao Tung University.

San-Chong Chyueh is currently a supervisor in the Department of Forensic Science, Ministry of Justice Investigation Bureau, Taiwan. He obtained his PhD degree from the Department of Chemistry, National Taiwan University in 1983. He is interested in mass spectrometry and analytic instruments.

Chien-Fu Chen is a Professor in the Institute of Applied Mechanics at National Taiwan University, Taiwan. He received the Ta-You Wu Memorial Award (2019), Futuretech Breakthrough Award (2019), and Futuretech Award (2020) from the National Science and Technology Council, Taiwan. Prof. Chen’s research focuses on miniaturized systems utilizing micro/nanomaterials and semiconductor fabrication techniques for environmental monitoring and biomedical point-of-care applications.
